title.zh: 卷四十五 蒯伍江息夫傳
title.en: Volume 45: Kuai , Wu, Jiang and Xifu
title.raw: 卷四十五 蒯伍江息夫傳 Volume 45: Kuai , Wu, Jiang and Xifu

sentences.zh: 卷四十五 蒯伍江息夫傳第十五

translations.literal: Scroll forty-five: Biographies of Kuai, Wu, Jiang, and Xifu, number fifteen.
translations.idiomatic: Volume 45: Biographies of Kuai Tong, Wu Bei, Jiang Chong, and Xifu Guang—Part 15.

sentences.zh: 蒯通，范陽人也，本與武帝同諱。

translations.literal: Kuai Tong was a man of Fanyang; originally he shared the same taboo name as Emperor Wu.
translations.idiomatic: Kuai Tong was from Fanyang; his original personal name had been the same character later tabooed under Emperor Wu.

sentences.zh: 楚漢初起，武臣略定趙地，號武信君。

translations.literal: When Chu and Han first rose, Wu Chen broadly pacified the territory of Zhao and styled himself Lord Wu Xin.
translations.idiomatic: At the outset of the struggle between Chu and Han, Wu Chen seized control of Zhao and took the title Lord Wu Xin.

sentences.zh: 通說范陽令徐公曰：「臣，范陽百姓蒯通也，竊閔公之將死，故吊之。

translations.literal: Tong addressed Magistrate Xu of Fanyang, saying: 'I, your servant, am Kuai Tong, a commoner of Fanyang; privately I pity that you are about to die, therefore I condole with you.'
translations.idiomatic: Kuai Tong said to Magistrate Xu of Fanyang: "I am Kuai Tong, a man of your county. I grieve that you stand on the brink of death, and I have come to offer my condolences."

sentences.zh: 雖然，賀公得通而生也。」

translations.literal: Even so, I congratulate you, sir, that having obtained Tong you will live.'
translations.idiomatic: Yet I also congratulate you: meeting me may be what saves your life."

sentences.zh: 徐公再拜曰：「何以吊之？」

translations.literal: Lord Xu twice bowed and said: 'With what do you condole me?'
translations.idiomatic: Magistrate Xu bowed twice and asked, "Why do you say I am about to die?"

sentences.zh: 通曰：「足下為令十餘年矣，殺人之父，孤人之子，斷人之足，黥人之首，甚眾。

translations.literal: Tong said: 'You, sir, have been magistrate more than ten years; you have killed people's fathers, orphaned people's sons, cut off people's feet, tattooed people's foreheads—very many.'
translations.idiomatic: Kuai Tong replied: "You have held this magistracy for more than ten years. You have put fathers to the sword, left sons fatherless, ordered mutilations and branding—far too many to count."

sentences.zh: 慈父孝子所以不敢事刃於公之腹者，畏秦法也。

translations.literal: The reason loving fathers and filial sons dare not apply the blade to your belly is that they fear Qin law.
translations.idiomatic: The only reason grieving fathers and dutiful sons have not plunged a knife into your gut is that they still feared the laws of Qin.

sentences.zh: 今天下大亂，秦政不施，然則慈父孝子將爭接刃於公之腹，以復其怨而成其名。

translations.literal: Now the realm is greatly in disorder; Qin government is not applied; then loving fathers and filial sons will compete to join blade to your belly, thereby avenging their grievances and completing their names.
translations.idiomatic: The realm is in chaos now, and Qin's punishments no longer hold them back. Those same fathers and sons will vie to run you through, to settle old scores and win a reputation for vengeance.

sentences.zh: 此通之所以吊者也。」

translations.literal: This is why Tong condoles with you.'
translations.idiomatic: That is why I said I mourned for you."

sentences.zh: 曰：「何以賀得子而生也？」

translations.literal: He said: 'With what do I congratulate you on obtaining the son and living?' (Some editions read 得通 for 得子, tying the reply to Kuai Tong's name.)
translations.idiomatic: Magistrate Xu asked, "Then why do you say my meeting you will save my life?"

sentences.zh: 曰：「趙武信君不知通不肖，使人候問其死生，通且見武信君而說之，曰：『必將戰勝而後略地，攻得而後下城，臣竊以為殆矣。

translations.literal: He said: 'Lord Wu Xin of Zhao, not knowing Tong is unworthy, sent men to inquire of my life or death; Tong will soon see Lord Wu Xin and persuade him, saying: "You are sure to conquer only then seize territory, attack and obtain only then take cities—your servant privately thinks this is perilous.'
translations.idiomatic: Kuai Tong said: "Lord Wu Xin of Zhao does not know my faults; he has sent to ask whether I still live. I am going to see him and urge this: 'If you insist on winning every battle before you take ground, on storming every wall before you accept surrender, I believe you court disaster.'

sentences.zh: 用臣之計，毋戰而略地，不攻而下城，傳檄而千里定，可乎？』

translations.literal: Employ your servant's plan: without battle seize territory, without attack take cities, transmit a proclamation and a thousand li are settled—may it be so?'
translations.idiomatic: Follow my counsel, and you can occupy territory without a fight, take cities without a siege, and bring a thousand li to heel with nothing more than a written summons—will you hear me out?'"

sentences.zh: 彼將曰：『何謂也？』

translations.literal: He will say: 'What does this mean?'
translations.idiomatic: He will ask what you mean by that."

sentences.zh: 臣因對曰：『范陽令宜整頓其士卒以守戰者也，怯而畏死，貪而好富貴，故欲以其城先下君。

translations.literal: Your servant therefore will answer, saying: 'The magistrate of Fanyang ought to be one who arrays his soldiers to defend and fight; he is timid and fears death, greedy and loves wealth and honor, therefore wishes to with his city first submit to you, lord.'
translations.idiomatic: You answer: 'The magistrate of Fanyang is the sort who should drill his troops for defense, but he is a coward who clings to life and a miser who craves rank. He wants to hand you the city first.'"

sentences.zh: 先下君而君不利之，則邊地之城皆將相告曰『范陽令先降而身死』，必將嬰城固守，皆為金城湯池，不可攻也。

translations.literal: If he first submits to you, lord, and you, lord, do not treat him favorably, then borderland cities will all mutually tell saying 'the magistrate of Fanyang first surrendered and personally died'; they will surely coil about the cities and firmly defend; all will become metal walls boiling moats, not able to be attacked.'
translations.idiomatic: If he surrenders first and you give him no reward, every garrison along the frontier will say, 'The magistrate of Fanyang yielded and was executed anyway.' They will bar their gates and fight to the last; each town will become a fortress you cannot storm."

sentences.zh: 為君計者，莫若以黃屋硃輪迎范陽令，使馳騖於燕、趙之郊，則邊城皆將相告曰『范陽令先下而身富貴』，必相率而降，猶如阪上走丸也。

translations.literal: For you, lord, planning, nothing is like using yellow canopy vermillion wheels to welcome the magistrate of Fanyang, causing him to gallop on the borders of Yan and Zhao, then border cities will all mutually tell saying 'the magistrate of Fanyang first descended and personally became wealthy and noble'; they will surely lead one another in surrendering, like on a slope rolling pellets.'
translations.idiomatic: Better plan: receive the magistrate of Fanyang in a ruler's carriage with the vermillion wheels of honor and parade him through Yan and Zhao. Then every border post will hear that the man who yielded first lives in wealth and rank. They will surrender in droves—like marbles racing down a slope."

sentences.zh: 此臣所謂傳檄而千里定者也。」

translations.literal: This is what your servant calls transmitting a proclamation and a thousand li being settled.'
translations.idiomatic: That is what I meant by settling a thousand li with a single proclamation.'"

sentences.zh: 徐公再拜，具車馬遣通。

translations.literal: Lord Xu twice bowed, prepared chariots and horses and dispatched Tong.
translations.idiomatic: Magistrate Xu bowed again, furnished a carriage and escort, and sent Kuai Tong on his way.

sentences.zh: 通遂以此說武臣。

translations.literal: Tong thereupon with this persuaded Wu Chen.
translations.idiomatic: Kuai Tong laid the same argument before Wu Chen.

sentences.zh: 武臣以車百乘、騎二百、侯印迎徐公。

translations.literal: Wu Chen with one hundred chariots, two hundred riders, and a marquis's seal welcomed Lord Xu.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Chen sent a hundred chariots, two hundred horsemen, and a marquis's seal to welcome Magistrate Xu.

sentences.zh: 燕、趙聞之，降者三十餘城。

translations.literal: Yan and Zhao heard it; those who surrendered were more than thirty cities.
translations.idiomatic: When word spread through Yan and Zhao, more than thirty cities submitted without a fight.

sentences.zh: 如通策焉。

translations.literal: It was as Tong's plan.
translations.idiomatic: Events unfolded exactly as Kuai Tong had predicted.

sentences.zh: 後漢將韓信虜魏王，破趙、代，降燕，定三國，引兵將東擊齊。

translations.literal: Afterwards Han general Han Xin captured the King of Wei, broke Zhao and Dai, subdued Yan, settled three states, led troops about to east attack Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Later, Han's general Han Xin took the King of Wei captive, crushed Zhao and Dai, forced Yan to yield, and consolidated three kingdoms before marching east against Qi.

sentences.zh: 未度平原，聞漢王使酈食其說下齊，信欲止。

translations.literal: Not yet having crossed Pingyuan, he heard the King of Han sent Li Yi Ji to persuade and bring down Qi; Xin wished to stop.
translations.idiomatic: Before he had crossed the plain of Pingyuan, he learned that the King of Han had sent Li Yiji to talk Qi into submission. Han Xin was ready to call off the campaign.

sentences.zh: 通說信曰：「將軍受詔擊齊，而漢獨發間使下齊，寧有詔止將軍乎？

translations.literal: Tong persuaded Xin, saying: 'The general received edict to strike Qi, yet Han alone dispatched a secret envoy to bring down Qi—is there possibly an edict stopping the general?'
translations.idiomatic: Kuai Tong said to him: "Your orders were to attack Qi. The court sent Li Yiji on a private mission to win Qi by diplomacy. Has any edict actually ordered you to halt?"

sentences.zh: 得以得無行！

translations.literal: How could you not go! (The transmitted graph sequence 得以得無行 is corrupt; parallel texts read 何得無行.)
translations.idiomatic: How can you possibly hold back now? You must march."

sentences.zh: 且酈生一士，伏軾掉三寸舌，下齊七十餘城，將軍將數萬之眾，乃下趙五十餘城。

translations.literal: Moreover Li Sheng one scholar, leaning on crossbar wielding three-cun tongue, brought down Qi more than seventy cities; the general leading several ten thousands of multitudes, only brought down Zhao more than fifty cities.
translations.idiomatic: Moreover, Li Yiji is a single scholar who leaned on his chariot rail and wagging a three-inch tongue took seventy Qi cities, while you commanded tens of thousands and barely wrested fifty-odd towns from Zhao.

sentences.zh: 為將數歲，反不如一豎儒之功乎！」

translations.literal: Being general several years, on the contrary not equal to one petty scholar's merit!'
translations.idiomatic: After years as a general, will you let your record be eclipsed by some pedant with a clever tongue?"

sentences.zh: 於是信然之，從其計，遂度河。

translations.literal: Thereupon Xin deemed it so, followed his plan, thereupon crossed the river.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin accepted the advice, followed Kuai Tong's plan, and crossed the Yellow River.

sentences.zh: 齊已聽酈生，即留之縱酒，罷備漢守禦。

translations.literal: Qi had already listened to Li Sheng, then detained him indulging wine, dismissed preparing Han defense.
translations.idiomatic: Qi had already taken Li Yiji at his word, kept him as a guest for feasting, and stood down the defenses on Han's border.

sentences.zh: 信因襲歷下軍，遂至臨菑。

translations.literal: Xin thereupon raided the army at Lixia, thereupon reached Linzi.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin struck the garrison at Lixia by surprise and drove straight to the capital, Linzi.

sentences.zh: 齊王以酈生為欺己而亨之，因敗走。

translations.literal: The King of Qi took Li Sheng as having deceived himself and boiled him, thereupon defeated fled.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Qi decided Li Yiji had betrayed him and had him boiled alive in a cauldron, then fled the field in defeat.

sentences.zh: 信遂定齊地，自立為齊假王。

translations.literal: Xin thereupon settled Qi territory, himself established as Qi acting king.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin secured Qi and declared himself acting king of Qi.

sentences.zh: 漢方困於滎陽，遣張良即立信為齊王，以安固之。

translations.literal: Han was just trapped at Yingyang, dispatched Zhang Liang immediately to establish Xin as King of Qi, thereby pacify and secure him.
translations.idiomatic: While Han was hard pressed at Yingyang, it sent Zhang Liang to confirm Han Xin as king of Qi in order to bind his loyalty.

sentences.zh: 項王亦遣武涉說信，欲與連和。

translations.literal: King Xiang also dispatched Wu She to persuade Xin, wishing with him to join in alliance.
translations.idiomatic: King Xiang Yu likewise sent Wu She to urge Han Xin toward an alliance.

sentences.zh: 蒯通知天下權在信，欲說信令背漢，乃先微感信曰：「僕嘗受相人之術，相君之面，不過封侯，又危而不安；

translations.literal: Kuai Tong knew the realm's power was in Xin, wished to persuade Xin ordering him to betray Han, thereupon first subtly move Xin, saying: 'Your servant once received face-reading art; reading your lordship's face, no more than enfeoffment as marquis, moreover perilous and not secure.'
translations.idiomatic: Kuai Tong saw that the balance of power rested with Han Xin and meant to persuade him to break with Han. He began obliquely: "I once studied physiognomy. Your face shows no higher fortune than a marquis's rank—and even that is fraught with danger."

sentences.zh: 相君之背，貴而不可言。」

translations.literal: Reading your lordship's back, noble and not able to be spoken.'
translations.idiomatic: Turn your back [i.e., rebel], and your eminence defies words."

sentences.zh: 信曰：「何謂也？」

translations.literal: Xin said: 'What does this mean?'
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin asked what he meant.

sentences.zh: 通因請間，曰：「天下初作難也，俊雄豪桀建號壹呼，天下之士雲合霧集，魚鱗雜襲，飄至風起。

translations.literal: Tong thereupon requested privacy, saying: 'When the realm first raised difficulty, handsome heroes mighty champions established titles one cry, the realm's gentlemen cloud joined fog gathered, fish scale mixed swarm, whirl arrive wind rise.'
translations.idiomatic: Kuai Tong asked leave to speak in private. "When rebellion first swept the land, every bold spirit who raised a banner drew followers like clouds and mist, packed tight as fish scales, swept along like windblown rain."

sentences.zh: 當此之時，憂在亡秦而已。

translations.literal: At this time, worry was in destroying Qin only.
translations.idiomatic: In those days the only fear was whether Qin could be overthrown.

sentences.zh: 今劉、項分爭，使人肝腦塗地，流離中野，不可勝數。

translations.literal: Now Liu and Xiang divide and contend, causing people liver brains smear earth, drift scatter mid wilderness, not able to be counted.
translations.idiomatic: Now Liu Bang and Xiang Yu tear the realm between them. Men lie with their brains dashed out on the field; refugees choke the roads beyond counting.

sentences.zh: 漢王將數十萬眾，距鞏、雒、岨山河，一日數戰，無尺寸之功，折北不救，敗滎陽，傷成皋，還走宛、葉之間，此所謂智勇俱困者也。

translations.literal: The King of Han leads several ten thousands multitudes, blocking Gong, Luo, obstructing mountains rivers, one day several battles, without inch foot merit, broken north not saved, defeated Yingyang, wounded Chenggao, returned fleeing between Wan and Ye—this is what is called wisdom and courage both trapped.'
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han has thrown army after army—hundreds of thousands—against the passes from Gong and Luoyang to the barrier of mountain and river. He fights day after day yet wins no ground; his lines collapse faster than they can be patched. He lost Yingyang, was bloodied at Chenggao, and fell back to stumble between Wan and Ye. That is what it means to be spent in wit and nerve alike.

sentences.zh: 楚人起彭城，轉斗逐北，至滎陽，乘利席勝，威震天下，然兵困於京、索之間，迫西山而不能進，三年於此矣。

translations.literal: Chu men rose from Pengcheng, turned fought pursued north, reached Yingyang, riding profit spreading victory, awe shook the realm, yet troops were trapped between Jing and Suo, pressed Western Mountain and not able to advance, three years at this already.'
translations.idiomatic: The Chu army marched out from Pengcheng, drove the enemy before it all the way to Yingyang, and rode its victories until its name shook the world—yet for three years now it has been pinned between Jing and Suo, blocked by the western hills, unable to advance another step.

sentences.zh: 銳氣挫於險塞，糧食盡於內藏，百姓罷極，無所歸命。

translations.literal: Sharp qi was thwarted at perilous passes; grain food exhausted in inner stores; common people exhausted extreme, nowhere to return mandate.'
translations.idiomatic: Their keen edge has been ground down on stubborn terrain; their granaries are empty; the people are at the end of their strength and see no lord worth dying for.

sentences.zh: 以臣料之，非天下賢聖，其勢固不能息天下之禍。

translations.literal: By your servant estimating it, unless the realm's worthy sage, its situation certainly cannot extinguish the realm's calamity.'
translations.idiomatic: In my judgment, unless some true sage appears, neither side can end this catastrophe.

sentences.zh: 當今之時，兩主縣命足下。

translations.literal: At the present time, the two lords suspend fate on your foot.'
translations.idiomatic: Today the lives of both kings hang on your decision.

sentences.zh: 足下為漢則漢勝。

translations.literal: You, sir, for Han then Han wins.'
translations.idiomatic: Throw your weight to Han, and Han wins.

sentences.zh: 與楚則楚勝。

translations.literal: Aid Chu then Chu wins.'
translations.idiomatic: Throw it to Chu, and Chu wins.

sentences.zh: 臣願披心腹，墮肝膽，效愚忠，恐足下不能用也。

translations.literal: Your servant wishes to split heart belly, drop liver gall, offer foolish loyalty, fearing you, sir, cannot use it.'
translations.idiomatic: I am ready to lay bare my heart and spill my guts in loyal counsel—though I doubt you will heed it.

sentences.zh: 方今為足下計，莫若兩利而俱存之，參分天下，鼎足而立，其勢莫敢先動。

translations.literal: Now for you, sir, planning, nothing is like both benefiting and together preserving them, tripartite divide the realm, tripod foot and stand; its situation none dares first move.'
translations.idiomatic: The soundest course for you now is to let both rivals survive while you profit: divide the empire in three and stand like a tripod leg—then neither side will dare move against you first.

sentences.zh: 夫以足下之賢聖，有甲兵之眾，據強齊，從燕、趙，出空虛之地以制其後，因民之欲，西鄉為百姓請命，天下孰敢不聽！

translations.literal: By means of you, sir's worthy sage, having armored soldiers multitudes, occupying strong Qi, following Yan and Zhao, emerging empty hollow lands thereby control their rear, following the people's desires, westward for the hundred surnames request life—who in the realm dares not listen!'
translations.idiomatic: With your gifts, your seasoned troops, all of Qi in your grasp, Yan and Zhao ready to follow, you could strike at their undefended rear and ride the people's longing for peace. March west in the name of the common folk, and who would dare defy you?

sentences.zh: 足下按齊國之故，有淮、泗之地，懷諸侯以德，深拱揖讓，則天下君王相率而朝齊矣。

translations.literal: You, sir, pressing Qi state's old, having Huai and Si lands, cherishing the feudal lords with virtue, deeply arching hands yielding deferentially, then the realm's kings lords will lead one another in court audience to Qi.'
translations.idiomatic: Hold fast to Qi's ancient heartland, add the basins of the Huai and Si, win the regional lords with kindness, and sit in calm courtesy on your throne: the kings of the realm will soon be lining up to pay homage at your court.

sentences.zh: 蓋聞『天與弗取，反受其咎；

translations.literal: I have heard: 'Heaven gives and not taking, on the contrary receives its blame.'
translations.idiomatic: The saying runs, 'What Heaven bestows and you refuse, turns to punishment.'

sentences.zh: 時至弗行，反受其殃』。

translations.literal: Time arrives and not acting, on the contrary receives its disaster.'
translations.idiomatic: When the moment comes and you fail to act, you invite disaster instead."

sentences.zh: 願足下孰圖之。」

translations.literal: I wish you, sir, carefully deliberate it.'
translations.idiomatic: I urge you to weigh this with care."

sentences.zh: 信曰：「漢遇我厚，吾豈可見利而背恩乎！」

translations.literal: Xin said: 'Han treats me thick; how can I see profit and betray grace!'
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin replied, "The King of Han has dealt generously with me. I cannot chase gain and cast gratitude aside."

sentences.zh: 通曰：「始常山王、成安君故相與為刎頸之交，及爭張黶、陳釋之事，常山王奉頭鼠竄，以歸漢王。

translations.literal: Tong said: 'At first the king of Changshan and the lord of Chengan were sworn friends to the death; when they quarreled over Zhang Yan and Chen Shi, the king of Changshan tucked his head like a rat and fled to the King of Han.'
translations.idiomatic: Kuai Tong said: "Zhang Er, king of Changshan, and Chen Yu, lord of Chengan, were sworn brothers who would die for each other—until they fell out over Zhang Yan and Chen Shi. Then Zhang Er fled like a cornered rat and threw himself on the King of Han's mercy."

sentences.zh: 借兵東下，戰於鄗北，成安君死於泜水之南，頭足異處。

translations.literal: He borrowed troops and marched east, fought north of Hao, and the lord of Chengan died south of the Zhi River, his head and body parted.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Er took Han troops east, met Chen Yu north of Hao, and left him dead south of the Zhi River, head severed from trunk."

sentences.zh: 此二人相與，天下之至□也，而卒相滅亡者，何也？

translations.literal: These two in their dealings reached the utmost in the realm (the character is missing in the received text), yet in the end destroyed each other—why?'
translations.idiomatic: Their friendship was as close as any in the world, yet they ended by destroying each other. Why?"

sentences.zh: 患生於多欲而人心難測也。

translations.literal: Calamity was born from many desires and people's hearts hard to fathom.'
translations.idiomatic: Because disaster grows from endless wants and the human heart cannot be read."

sentences.zh: 今足下行忠信以交於漢王，必不能固於二君之相與也，而事多大於張黶、陳釋之事者，故臣以為足下必漢王之不危足下，過矣。

translations.literal: Now you, sir, practice loyalty and good faith toward the King of Han, yet you cannot be more firmly bound than those two lords were to each other, while the matters at stake are greater than the quarrel over Zhang Yan and Chen Shi; therefore your servant thinks that for you, sir, to be sure the King of Han will never endanger you, sir, is a grave error.'
translations.idiomatic: You stake everything on loyalty toward the King of Han, but you cannot be tighter with him than Zhang Er and Chen Yu were with each other, and what stands between you and Liu Bang is already graver than their feud over Zhang Yan and Chen Shi. To be certain he will never move against you is a dangerous delusion."

sentences.zh: 大夫種存亡越，伯句踐，立功名而身死。

translations.literal: Grandee Zhong saved Yue from extinction, made King Goujian a hegemon, won merit and fame, yet lost his own life.'
translations.idiomatic: Minister Zhong rescued Yue from destruction, raised King Goujian to overlordship, built immortal renown—and was put to death for it."

sentences.zh: 語曰：『野禽殫，走犬亨；

translations.literal: The saying says: 'When wild fowl are exhausted, running dogs are boiled.'
translations.idiomatic: The proverb says, 'When the last bird is shot, the hunting dogs go into the pot.'

sentences.zh: 敵國破，謀臣亡。』

translations.literal: Enemy state broken, plotting ministers perish.'
translations.idiomatic: When the rival state falls, the strategist perishes with it.'"

sentences.zh: 故以交友言之，則不過張王與成安君；

translations.literal: Therefore by friendship speaking, then not surpassing Zhang Wang and Lord Chengan.'
translations.idiomatic: In friendship you go no deeper than Zhang Er and Chen Yu once did.

sentences.zh: 以忠臣言之，則不過大夫種。

translations.literal: By loyal minister speaking, then not surpassing Grandee Zhong.'
translations.idiomatic: In loyal service you have done no more than Zhong of Yue.

sentences.zh: 此二者，宜足以觀矣。

translations.literal: These two kinds, ought to be sufficient to observe.'
translations.idiomatic: Those two examples should be lesson enough."

sentences.zh: 願足下深慮之。

translations.literal: I wish you, sir, deeply consider it.'
translations.idiomatic: Think on it long and hard."

sentences.zh: 且臣聞之，勇略震主者身危，功蓋天下者不賞。

translations.literal: Moreover your servant has heard: courage strategy that shakes the lord, body is perilous; merit that covers the realm, not rewarded.'
translations.idiomatic: I have also heard that when courage and cunning awe one's sovereign, the man is already in peril, and when his achievements overshadow the realm, no reward can suffice.

sentences.zh: 足下涉西河，虜魏王，禽夏說，下井陘，誅成安君之罪，以令於趙，脅燕定齊，南摧楚人之兵數十萬眾，遂斬龍且，西鄉以報，此所謂功無二於天下，略不出出者也。

translations.literal: You, sir, crossed the western Yellow River, captured the king of Wei, seized Xia Shuo, took the Jingxing defile, punished the crimes of the lord of Chengan and brought Zhao to heel, coerced Yan and settled Qi, southward shattered several hundred thousand Chu troops, beheaded Long Ju, and turned west to report—this is what is called merit second to none under Heaven and strategy not matched in a generation (the received text reads 略不出出, emended by commentators to 略不世出).'
translations.idiomatic: You crossed the west-bank Yellow River, took the king of Wei, captured Xia Shuo, forced Jingxing, avenged Chen Yu's wrongs and bent Zhao to your will, overawed Yan and pacified Qi, shattered Chu hosts in the hundreds of thousands, struck down Long Ju, and wheeled west to answer the King of Han—achievements without parallel and stratagem seldom seen in any age.

sentences.zh: 今足下挾不賞之功，戴震主之威，歸楚，楚人不信；

translations.literal: Now you, sir, clutching not-rewardable merit, wearing awe-shaking-lord's might, return Chu, Chu men will not trust.'
translations.idiomatic: Yet you now hold achievements too great to be rewarded and a reputation that terrifies your sovereign. If you went over to Chu, the Chu camp would never trust you.

sentences.zh: 歸漢，漢人震恐。

translations.literal: Return Han, Han men tremble in fear.'
translations.idiomatic: If you stay with Han, every minister at court flinches at your shadow.

sentences.zh: 足下欲持是安歸乎？

translations.literal: You, sir, wish to holding this, where peacefully return?'
translations.idiomatic: Where can you take that standing and hope to remain safe?"

sentences.zh: 夫勢在人臣之位，而有高天下之名，切為足下危之。」

translations.literal: Now situation in human minister's position, yet having high the realm's name, truly for you, sir, I fear it.'
translations.idiomatic: To remain a subject yet tower above every name in the empire—sir, I tremble for you."

sentences.zh: 信曰：「生且休矣，吾將念之。」

translations.literal: Xin said: 'Sir, for now rest; I will ponder it.'
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin said, "Leave it for now. I need time to think."

sentences.zh: 數日，通復說曰：「聽者，事之候也；

translations.literal: Several days, Tong again persuaded, saying: 'Listening is affairs' sign.'
translations.idiomatic: A few days later Kuai Tong resumed: "To heed counsel is to read the signs of events."

sentences.zh: 計者，存亡之機也。

translations.literal: Planning is survival extinction's pivot.'
translations.idiomatic: To plan well is to grasp the hinge of survival or ruin."

sentences.zh: 夫隨廝養之役者，失萬乘之權；

translations.literal: Those who follow menial servants' service, lose ten-thousands chariots' power.'
translations.idiomatic: The man who stoops to a groom's errands forfeits an emperor's leverage."

sentences.zh: 守儋石之祿者，闕卿相之位。

translations.literal: Those who guard dan-shi salary, lack minister chancellor's position.'
translations.idiomatic: The clerk who clings to a pittance of pay will never reach ministerial rank."

sentences.zh: 計誠知之，而決弗敢行者，百事之禍也。

translations.literal: Planning truly knowing it, yet deciding dare not act—this is hundred affairs' calamity.'
translations.idiomatic: To see the right course yet shrink from acting invites every kind of disaster."

sentences.zh: 故猛虎之猶與，不如蜂蠆之致□；

translations.literal: Therefore a fierce tiger that hesitates is not the equal of bees and wasps that deliver their sting (the final character is missing in the received text).'
translations.idiomatic: A tiger that cannot strike is less to be feared than wasps and scorpions that do."

sentences.zh: 孟賁之狐疑，不如童子之必至。

translations.literal: Meng Ben's doubt, not equal to child's must arrive.'
translations.idiomatic: Meng Ben the strongman, if he wavers, is less use than a boy who sees a thing through."

sentences.zh: 此言貴能行之也。

translations.literal: This speaks of valuing able to act.'
translations.idiomatic: The point is that resolve must show in deeds."

sentences.zh: 夫功者，難成而易敗；

translations.literal: Now merit, hard to complete and easy to defeat.'
translations.idiomatic: Merit is hard won and easily lost."

sentences.zh: 時者，難值而易失。

translations.literal: Opportunity, hard to meet and easy to lose.'
translations.idiomatic: The moment is hard to catch and quick to slip away."

sentences.zh: 『時乎時，不再來。』

translations.literal: 'Time oh time, not again come.'
translations.idiomatic: 'Time, time—once gone it does not return.'"

sentences.zh: 願足下無疑臣之計。」

translations.literal: I wish you, sir, not doubt your servant's plan.'
translations.idiomatic: Do not doubt my counsel."

sentences.zh: 信猶與不忍背漢，又自以功多，漢不奪我齊，遂謝通。

translations.literal: Xin hesitated could not bear betray Han, also self thought merit many, Han would not seize my Qi, thereupon declined Tong.'
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin still could not bring himself to betray Han. He told himself his deeds were too many for Liu Bang to strip him of Qi, and he dismissed Kuai Tong.

sentences.zh: 通說不聽，惶恐，乃陽狂為巫。

translations.literal: Tong's persuasion not listened, fearful, thereupon feigned madness as shaman.'
translations.idiomatic: Seeing that Han Xin would not listen, Kuai Tong took fright and pretended madness, passing himself off as a medium.

sentences.zh: 天下既定，後信以罪廢為淮陰侯，謀反被誅，臨死歎曰：「悔不用蒯通之言，死於女子之手！」

translations.literal: The realm already settled, afterwards Xin because crime deposed as Marquis of Huaiyin, plotting rebellion was executed, facing death sighed, saying: 'I regret not using Kuai Tong's words, dying in woman's hands!'
translations.idiomatic: After the realm was pacified, Han Xin was degraded to marquis of Huaiyin for an offense, then executed for conspiracy. On the scaffold he cried, "I rue the day I ignored Kuai Tong—to die at a woman's hand!"

sentences.zh: 高帝曰：「是齊辯士蒯通。」

translations.literal: The High Emperor said: 'This is Qi's disputing scholar Kuai Tong.'
translations.idiomatic: The High Emperor said, "That is Kuai Tong, the sharp-tongued counselor of Qi."

sentences.zh: 乃詔齊召蒯通。

translations.literal: Thereupon he issued an edict summoning Kuai Tong from Qi.
translations.idiomatic: He then sent an edict to Qi ordering Kuai Tong brought to court.

sentences.zh: 通至，上欲亨之，曰：「昔教韓信反，何也？」

translations.literal: Tong arrived, the sovereign wished to boil him, saying: 'Formerly you taught Han Xin rebel—why?'
translations.idiomatic: When Kuai Tong arrived, the emperor meant to boil him alive and demanded, "Why did you once urge Han Xin to revolt?"

sentences.zh: 通曰：「狗各吠非其主。

translations.literal: Tong said: 'Dogs each bark not their master.'
translations.idiomatic: Kuai Tong replied, "Every hound barks at strangers, not at its own master."

sentences.zh: 當彼時，臣獨知齊王韓信，非知陛下也。

translations.literal: At that time, your servant alone knew King of Qi Han Xin, not knew Your Majesty.'
translations.idiomatic: In those days I knew only my king, Han Xin of Qi; I did not know Your Majesty."

sentences.zh: 且秦失其鹿，天下共逐之，高材者先得。

translations.literal: Moreover Qin lost its deer, the realm together chased it, high talent ones first obtained.'
translations.idiomatic: Besides, when Qin dropped the hunt, every contender in the realm chased the prize, and the ablest reached it first."

sentences.zh: 天下匈匈，爭欲為陛下所為，顧力不能，可殫誅邪！」

translations.literal: The realm clamorous, competing wishing to do what Your Majesty did, only strength not able—can exhaust execute them!'
translations.idiomatic: The empire seethed with men who would gladly have done what you did had they had the strength—would you boil every one of them?"

sentences.zh: 上乃赦之。

translations.literal: The sovereign thereupon pardoned him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor spared him.

sentences.zh: 至齊悼惠王理，曹參為相，禮下賢人，請通為客。

translations.literal: When it came to King Daohui of Qi (the character 理 in the received text is likely an error for 時, "the time of"), Cao Can was chancellor; he honored worthy men and invited Tong as a client.
translations.idiomatic: In the days of King Daohui of Qi, while Cao Can held the chancellorship, Cao treated worthy men with deference and took Kuai Tong into his household as a guest adviser.

sentences.zh: 初，齊王田榮怨項羽，謀舉兵畔之，劫齊士，不與者死。

translations.literal: At first King Tian Rong of Qi resented Xiang Yu, plotted to raise troops and rebel against him, coerced the gentlemen of Qi, and put to death any who would not join.
translations.idiomatic: At first King Tian Rong of Qi nursed a grudge against Xiang Yu, schemed to rise in arms against him, dragooned the scholars of Qi, and executed whoever refused to follow.

sentences.zh: 齊處士東郭先生、梁石君在劫中，強從。

translations.literal: The Qi recluses Mr. Dongguo and Liang Shijun were among those seized in the coercion and were forced to follow.
translations.idiomatic: The Qi recluses Dongguo Xiansheng and Liang Shijun were caught in that dragnet and marched along under duress.

sentences.zh: 及田榮敗，二人醜之，相與入深山隱居。

translations.literal: When Tian Rong was defeated, the two men were ashamed of it; together they entered the deep mountains and lived in hiding.
translations.idiomatic: After Tian Rong fell, both men were ashamed of what they had done and withdrew together into the mountains to live in seclusion.

sentences.zh: 客謂通曰：「先生之於曹相國，拾遺舉過，顯賢進能，齊功莫若先生者。

translations.literal: A retainer said to Tong: 'In relation to Chancellor Cao the minister of state, you pick up what was left out and expose faults, display the worthy and advance the capable—among achievements in Qi none matches the master.'
translations.idiomatic: A client said to Kuai Tong, "For Chancellor Cao you have supplied what was missing, called out missteps, brought worthy men to light, and promoted the able—no one in Qi has served him better than you."

sentences.zh: 先生知梁石君、東孝先生世俗所不及，何不進之於相國乎？」

translations.literal: The master knows that Liang Shijun and Mr. Dongxiao are beyond what the vulgar world can match—why not advance them to the minister of state?' (Many editions read the second recluse's name as Dongguo Xiansheng.)
translations.idiomatic: You know that Liang Shijun and Dongguo Xiansheng—as copyists here style him, Dongxiao—are men the common run cannot match. Why not put their names before the chancellor?"

sentences.zh: 通曰：「諾。

translations.literal: Tong said: 'Yes.'
translations.idiomatic: "Very well," said Kuai Tong.

sentences.zh: 臣之裡婦，與裡之諸母相善也。

translations.literal: A woman of my hamlet was on good terms with the matrons of the hamlet.
translations.idiomatic: There was a neighbor woman in my lane who was close to the older women there.

sentences.zh: 裡婦夜亡肉，姑以為盜，怒而逐之。

translations.literal: The hamlet wife lost meat by night; the mother-in-law thought her a thief, was angry and drove her out.
translations.idiomatic: One night meat went missing from her house; her mother-in-law took her for a thief, flew into a rage, and drove her away.

sentences.zh: 婦晨去，過所善諸母，語以事而謝之。

translations.literal: The woman left in the morning, passed by the matrons she was friendly with, told them the affair and apologized.
translations.idiomatic: At dawn she left, stopped by the matrons who were fond of her, explained what had happened, and took her leave.

sentences.zh: 裡母曰：『女安行，我今令而家追女矣。』

translations.literal: A hamlet matron said: 'Go safely; I will now order your household to chase you.'
translations.idiomatic: One of the matrons told her, "Walk on without fear—I have already sent word that your family will come after you."

sentences.zh: 即束縕請火於亡肉家，曰：『昨暮夜，犬得肉，爭鬥相殺，請火治之。』

translations.literal: Then she bundled hemp rope and begged fire from the household that had lost meat, saying: 'Last night the dogs got meat, fought and killed each other; I beg fire to treat them.'
translations.idiomatic: She twisted hemp into a slow-burning wick, went to the house that had lost the meat, and borrowed a light, saying, "Last night our dogs fought over a piece of meat until they killed each other; I need a coal to tend to them."

sentences.zh: 亡肉家遽追呼其婦。

translations.literal: The meat-losing household hastily ran after, calling their daughter-in-law.
translations.idiomatic: The household that had lost the meat rushed out shouting for their daughter-in-law to come home.

sentences.zh: 故里母非談說之士也，束縕乞火非還婦之道也，然物有相感，事有適可。

translations.literal: Therefore the hamlet matron was not a persuading rhetorician; bundling rope to beg fire was not the way to return a daughter-in-law—yet things have mutual resonance; affairs have what is fitting.
translations.idiomatic: That matron was no trained rhetorician, and borrowing fire with a hemp wick was no orthodox way to fetch a wife back—yet when circumstances strike the right note, the simplest device can move the world.

sentences.zh: 臣請乞火於曹相國。」

translations.literal: Your servant requests to beg fire from Chancellor Cao the minister of state.'
translations.idiomatic: I mean to borrow that same kind of fire from Chancellor Cao."

sentences.zh: 乃見相國曰：「婦人有夫死三日而嫁者，有幽居守寡不出門者，足下即欲求婦，何取？」

translations.literal: Then he saw the minister of state and said: 'Among women there are those whose husbands die and who remarry after three days, and those who dwell in seclusion as widows and do not go out of the gate—if you wish to take a wife, which do you choose?'
translations.idiomatic: He went in to see Cao and asked, "Some widows remarry three days after their husband dies; others shut their doors and keep widow's seclusion. If you were choosing a wife, which sort would you take?"

sentences.zh: 曰：「取不嫁者。」

translations.literal: He said: 'Take one who does not remarry.'
translations.idiomatic: "The one who will not remarry," said Cao.

sentences.zh: 通曰：「然則求臣亦猶是也，彼東郭先生、梁石君，齊之俊士也，隱居不嫁，未嘗卑節下意以求仕也。

translations.literal: Tong said: 'Then seeking ministers is likewise so—they, Mr. Dongguo and Liang Shijun, are outstanding scholars of Qi, dwelling in seclusion like not remarrying, never having lowered their integrity or humbled their will to seek office.
translations.idiomatic: Kuai Tong said, "Choosing a minister is the same. Dongguo Xiansheng and Liang Shijun are the finest talents in Qi; they live in retirement as chastely as a widow who refuses a second marriage and have never debased their integrity to chase an office."

sentences.zh: 願足下使人禮之。」

translations.literal: I wish you would send men to treat them with ritual courtesy.'
translations.idiomatic: Send messengers to honor them with proper ceremony."

sentences.zh: 曹相國曰：「敬受命。」

translations.literal: Chancellor Cao the minister of state said: 'I respectfully receive the command.'
translations.idiomatic: Chancellor Cao bowed and said, "Your advice is accepted."

sentences.zh: 皆以為上賓。

translations.literal: Both were treated as upper guests.
translations.idiomatic: Both men were received as guests of the highest rank.

sentences.zh: 通論戰國時說士權變，亦自序其說，凡八十一首，號曰《雋永》。

translations.literal: Tong discussed the persuaders' power and shifts in Warring States times, also himself prefaced his persuasions, altogether eighty-one pieces, titled Juan Yong (隽永).
translations.idiomatic: Kuai Tong wrote on the stratagems of the Warring States persuaders, prefaced his own speeches, and collected eighty-one pieces under the title Juan Yong.

sentences.zh: 初，通善齊人安其生，安其生嘗干項羽，羽不能用其策。

translations.literal: At first Tong was on good terms with the Qi man Ansheng; Ansheng once importuned Xiang Yu, but Yu could not use his plans.
translations.idiomatic: Kuai Tong had long been friendly with the Qi scholar Ansheng, who once pressed his counsel on Xiang Yu—counsel Yu never adopted.

sentences.zh: 而項羽欲封此兩人，兩人卒不肯受。

translations.literal: Yet Xiang Yu wished to enfeoff these two men; in the end both refused to accept.
translations.idiomatic: Even so, Xiang Yu wanted to ennoble them both, and both steadfastly declined.

sentences.zh: 伍被，楚人也。

translations.literal: Wu Pi was a man of Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Pi was a native of Chu.

sentences.zh: 或言其先伍子胥後也。

translations.literal: Some say his ancestors were descendants of Wu Zixu.
translations.idiomatic: Some say his clan descended from Wu Zixu.

sentences.zh: 被以材能稱，為淮南中郎。

translations.literal: Pi was praised for talent and ability, and served as palace gentleman of Huainan.
translations.idiomatic: Celebrated for talent, he rose to palace gentleman in the court of the king of Huainan.

sentences.zh: 是時淮南王安好術學，折節下士，招致英雋以百數，被為冠首。

translations.literal: At that time Prince Huainan Liu An loved arts and learning, lowered himself to treat scholars well, and summoned several hundred outstanding men; Pi was crown of the head among them.
translations.idiomatic: King An of Huainan doted on scholarship, humbled himself before men of learning, and drew several hundred brilliant retainers to his court; Wu Pi stood first among them.

sentences.zh: 久之，淮南王陰有邪謀，被數微諫。

translations.literal: After a long time the Prince of Huainan secretly harbored evil plots; Pi several times subtly remonstrated.
translations.idiomatic: In time the king began to nurse treasonable designs, and Wu Pi remonstrated again and again in oblique terms.

sentences.zh: 後王坐東宮，召被欲與計事，呼之曰：「將軍上。」

translations.literal: Later the king sat in the Eastern Palace, summoned Pi wishing to consult on plans, called to him: 'General, ascend.'
translations.idiomatic: Later the king received him in the Eastern Palace to discuss his schemes and called up the steps, "Come closer, general."

sentences.zh: 被曰：「王安得亡國之言乎？

translations.literal: Pi said: 'How can the king obtain words of a perishing state?'
translations.idiomatic: Wu Pi said, "Why speak the language of a doomed kingdom?"

sentences.zh: 昔子胥諫吳王，吳王不用，乃曰『臣今見麋鹿游姑蘇之台也。』

translations.literal: Formerly Zixu remonstrated the King of Wu; the King of Wu did not use him, and he said: 'Your servant will now see deer roaming on the terrace of Gusu.'
translations.idiomatic: When Zixu warned the king of Wu and went unheeded, he said, "I shall live to see deer grazing on the terraces of Gusu."

sentences.zh: 今臣亦將見宮中生荊棘，露沾衣也。」

translations.literal: Now your servant will also see brambles growing in the palace, dew wetting the robes.'
translations.idiomatic: I shall yet see brambles springing in your halls and the dew of mourning soaking your robes."

sentences.zh: 於是王怒，系被父母，囚之三月。

translations.literal: Thereupon the king was angry, arrested Pi's parents, imprisoned him three months.
translations.idiomatic: The king flew into a rage, seized Wu Pi's parents as hostages, and kept him in chains for three months.

sentences.zh: 王復召被曰：「將軍許寡人乎？」

translations.literal: The king again summoned Pi, saying: 'General, do you consent to me?'
translations.idiomatic: When he summoned Wu Pi again he asked, "Will you stand with me now, general?"

sentences.zh: 被曰：「不，臣將為大王畫計耳。

translations.literal: Pi said: 'No—your servant will for the great king draw out a plan.'
translations.idiomatic: Wu Pi answered, "No—but I can still lay out a plan for Your Highness."

sentences.zh: 臣聞陪者聽於無聲，明者見於未形，故聖人萬舉而萬全。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that those who hear acutely listen in silence, the clear-sighted see before forms take shape; therefore the sage ten thousand times acts and ten thousand times is secure.' (The first word of the received text is corrupt; commentators emend it to a binome meaning acute hearing.)
translations.idiomatic: They whose ears are sharp catch truth in silence; they whose eyes are clear read fate before it takes shape. That is why the sage is never caught unprepared. The manuscript's opening word should mean acute hearing, not dull company."

sentences.zh: 文王壹動而功顯萬世，列為三王，所謂因天心以動作者也。」

translations.literal: King Wen one movement and merit displayed ten thousand generations, ranked among the Three Kings—what is called acting in accord with Heaven's heart.'
translations.idiomatic: King Wen made one decisive move and his fame has lasted ten thousand generations among the Three Kings—because he moved in step with Heaven's will."

sentences.zh: 王曰：「方今漢庭治乎？

translations.literal: The king said: 'Is the Han court at present well governed'
translations.idiomatic: The king asked, "Is the Han court well governed"

sentences.zh: 亂乎？」

translations.literal: or in disorder?'
translations.idiomatic: or in chaos today?"

sentences.zh: 被曰：「天下治。」

translations.literal: Pi said: 'The realm is well governed.'
translations.idiomatic: "It is well governed," said Wu Pi.

sentences.zh: 王不說，曰：「公何言治也？」

translations.literal: The king was displeased and said: 'Why do you say it is well governed?'
translations.idiomatic: The king scowled. "Why call it well governed?"

sentences.zh: 被對曰：「被竊觀朝廷，君臣、父子、夫婦、長幼之序皆得其理，上之舉錯遵古之道，風俗紀綱未有所缺。

translations.literal: Pi replied: 'Pi has privately observed the court: the order of lord and minister, father and son, husband and wife, elder and younger is all obtained in its principle; the ruler's measures follow the ways of antiquity; customs and discipline show no lack.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Pi replied, "I have watched the court closely. The bonds between ruler and minister, father and son, husband and wife, elder and younger all hold as they should; the throne follows the model of the ancients; custom and law are intact.

sentences.zh: 重裝富賈周流天下，道無不通，交易之道行。

translations.literal: Heavily laden rich merchants circulate through the realm; no road is blocked; the way of exchange prevails.
translations.idiomatic: Rich merchants with heavy wagons cross the empire unhindered, and trade runs everywhere it should.

sentences.zh: 南越賓服，羌、僰貢獻，東甌入朝，廣長榆，開朔方，匈奴折傷。

translations.literal: Nanyue submits as guest; Qiang and Bo offer tribute; Eastern Ou enters court; they extended Changyu, opened Shuofang, the Xiongnu were broken and wounded.
translations.idiomatic: Nanyue has accepted vassal status, the Qiang and Bo peoples send tribute, Eastern Ou pays court, the northern frontier runs from Changyu to Shuofang, and the Xiongnu have been broken on the field.

sentences.zh: 雖未及古太平時，然猶為治。」

translations.literal: Though not yet at the ancient age of Great Peace, still it counts as order.'
translations.idiomatic: We are not yet in the golden age of high antiquity, but this is still an ordered realm."

sentences.zh: 王怒，被謝死罪。

translations.literal: The king was angry; Pi begged forgiveness for a capital crime.
translations.idiomatic: The king's fury flared; Wu Pi threw himself on the ground and begged to be spared for lese-majeste.

sentences.zh: 王又曰：「山東即有變，漢必使大將軍將而制山東，公以為大將軍何如人也？」

translations.literal: The king also said: 'If east of the mountains there should be disturbance, Han will surely send the Grand General to lead troops and control the east—what kind of man do you think the Grand General is?'
translations.idiomatic: The king pressed him again. "If trouble breaks out east of the passes, the court will surely send the Grand General to crush it. What manner of man is he, in your eyes?"

sentences.zh: 被曰：「臣所善黃義，從大將軍擊匈奴，言大將軍遇士大夫以禮，與士卒有恩，眾皆樂為用。

translations.literal: Pi said: 'Your servant's friend Huang Yi followed the Grand General to strike the Xiongnu; he said the Grand General treats gentlemen with courtesy, has kindness toward soldiers, and the hosts are all glad to serve him.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Pi said, "My friend Huang Yi campaigned under the Grand General against the Xiongnu. He says the commander treats scholars with respect, shares every hardship with the ranks, and the whole army is eager to die for him.

sentences.zh: 騎上下山如飛，材力絕人如此，數將習兵，未易當也。

translations.literal: His cavalry ascends and descends hills like flying; his strength surpasses other men thus; he has repeatedly led troops versed in war—not easy to withstand.
translations.idiomatic: His horsemen fly up and down hills; his own strength is superhuman; he has led veteran hosts again and again—no easy foe."

sentences.zh: 及謁者曹梁使長安來，言大將軍號令明，當敵勇，常為士卒先；

translations.literal: And when attendant Cao Liang came from Chang'an, he said the Grand General's commands and orders are clear, facing the foe he is brave, always takes the lead before the soldiers;
translations.idiomatic: Cao Liang the usher, back from Chang'an, says his orders are lucid, his courage in battle legendary—he always fights in the van.

sentences.zh: 須士卒休，乃捨；

translations.literal: he waits until the soldiers rest, then lodges;
translations.idiomatic: He will not take quarters until the men have rested.

sentences.zh: 穿井得水，乃敢飲；

translations.literal: digs a well and gets water, then dares to drink;
translations.idiomatic: He will not drink until a well has been dug and the water proved safe.

sentences.zh: 軍罷，士卒已逾河，乃度。

translations.literal: when the army halts, the soldiers have already crossed the river, then he crosses.
translations.idiomatic: When the army disengages, he does not cross the river until the last soldier is over."

sentences.zh: 皇太后所賜金錢，盡以賞賜。

translations.literal: The gold and cash granted by the empress dowager he entirely used to reward and bestow.
translations.idiomatic: The gold the empress dowager sends him he passes straight to the troops as bounty.

sentences.zh: 雖古名將不過也。」

translations.literal: Even ancient famous generals were not better than this.'
translations.idiomatic: The commanders of old did no more than that."

sentences.zh: 王曰：「夫蓼太子知略不世出，非常人也，以為漢廷公卿列侯皆如沐猴而冠耳。」

translations.literal: The king said: 'Prince Liu Bi of Wu (the received text writes 蓼太子) knew stratagem not emerging in a generation, was an extraordinary man; he thought Han court dukes, ministers, and marquises were all like monkeys in caps.'
translations.idiomatic: The king said, "Liu Bi, king of Wu, was a strategist without peer; he used to say every minister and noble at court was a monkey in a hat."

sentences.zh: 被曰：「獨先刺大將軍，乃可舉事。」

translations.literal: Pi said: 'Only first assassinate the Grand General, then you can raise the affair.'
translations.idiomatic: "Then you must kill the Grand General first," said Wu Pi. "Only then can you move."

sentences.zh: 王復問被曰：「公以為吳舉兵非邪？」

translations.literal: The king again asked Pi: 'Do you think Wu's raising troops was wrong?'
translations.idiomatic: The king asked again whether Prince Liu Bi's rebellion had been wrong.

sentences.zh: 被曰：「非也。

translations.literal: Pi said: 'It was wrong.
translations.idiomatic: "It was a fatal mistake," said Wu Pi.

sentences.zh: 夫吳王賜號為劉氏祭酒，受几杖而不朝，王四郡之眾，地方數千里，采山銅以為錢，煮海水以為鹽，伐江陵之木以為船，國富民眾，行珍寶，賂諸侯，與七國合從，舉兵而西，破大梁，敗狐父，奔走而還，為越所禽，死於丹徒，頭足異處，身滅祀絕，為天下戮。

translations.literal: The King of Wu was granted title as Liu family's libationer, received stool and staff and did not attend court, had the multitudes of four commanderies, territory several thousand li square, mined mountain copper for coin, boiled sea water for salt, cut Jiangling wood for ships, state rich people many, circulated treasures, bribed feudal lords, joined vertical alliance with seven states, raised troops westward, broke Daliang, defeated Hufu, fled back in flight, was captured by Yue, died at Dantu, head and feet different places, body extinguished sacrifices cut off, made laughingstock for the realm.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Wu bore the Liu surname's honor of chief mourner, leaned on the gift of staff and stool to skip court, ruled four commanderies across thousands of li, coined money from his copper hills and salt from his boiling pans, built a fleet from Jiangling timber, grew rich and strong, bought allies among the kingdoms, joined the seven-state league, marched west, took Daliang, broke the army at Hufu, then fled headlong until Yue ran him down at Dantu—head severed from body, line extinguished, a cautionary tale for the world.

sentences.zh: 夫以吳眾不能成功者，何也？

translations.literal: That with Wu's multitudes they could not succeed—why?'
translations.idiomatic: Why, with all those hosts, did he still fail?"

sentences.zh: 誠逆天違眾而不見時也。」

translations.literal: Truly they opposed Heaven, went against the multitude, and did not perceive the moment.'
translations.idiomatic: Because he defied Heaven, alienated the people, and misread the times."

sentences.zh: 王曰：「男子之所死者，一言耳。

translations.literal: The king said: 'What makes a man willing to die is one word.
translations.idiomatic: The king said, "A man will die for a single word of honor.

sentences.zh: 且吳何知反？

translations.literal: Moreover how did Wu know to rebel?'
translations.idiomatic: Besides, how could Wu have known any better than to rebel?"

sentences.zh: 漢將一日過成皋者四十餘人。

translations.literal: Han generals in one day passed Chenggao more than forty men.
translations.idiomatic: In a single day more than forty Han generals rode through Chenggao.

sentences.zh: 今我令緩先要成皋之口，周被下穎川兵塞轘轅、伊闕之道，陳定發南陽兵守武關，河南太守獨有雒陽耳，何足憂？

translations.literal: Now if I order Huan first to hold the mouth of Chenggao, Zhou Bei to descend with Yingchuan troops and block Huanyuan and Yique roads, Chen Ding to raise Nanyang troops and guard Wuguan—the Grand Administrator of Henan alone has Luoyang; what is there to worry about?'
translations.idiomatic: If I put Huan at the throat of Chenggao, send Zhou Bei down the Yingchuan corridor to seal Huanyuan and Yique, and Chen Ding up from Nanyang to hold Wuguan, the governor of Henan is left with Luoyang alone—what is there to fear?"

sentences.zh: 然此北尚有臨晉關、河東、上黨與河內、趙國界者通谷數行。

translations.literal: Yet to the north there are still Linyi Pass, Hedong, Shangdang, and borders with Henei and Zhao—several routes through communicating valleys.
translations.idiomatic: North of that line lie Linyi Pass, Hedong, Shangdang, and the Henei border with Zhao—half a dozen defiles still open.

sentences.zh: 人言『絕成皋之道，天下不通』。

translations.literal: Men say: "Block the road to Chenggao and the realm cannot communicate."'
translations.idiomatic: Men say, "Cut Chenggao and the empire is split in two," yet the map tells another story."

sentences.zh: 據三川之險，招天下之兵，公以為何如？」

translations.literal: Occupy the peril of the three river region, summon the realm's hosts—what do you think?'
translations.idiomatic: Seize the choke points of the three river basins and call the realm to arms—do you truly think that enough?"

sentences.zh: 被曰：「臣見其禍，未見其福也。」

translations.literal: Pi said: 'Your servant sees its misfortune, not its blessing.'
translations.idiomatic: "I see nothing ahead but ruin," said Wu Pi.

sentences.zh: 後漢逮淮南王孫建，系治之。

translations.literal: Later Han arrested the Prince of Huainan's grandson Jian and investigated him.
translations.idiomatic: Soon afterward Han authorities seized the king's grandson Liu Jian for questioning.

sentences.zh: 王恐陰事洩，謂被曰：「事至，吾欲遂發。

translations.literal: The king feared his secret matters would leak; he said to Pi: 'When the matter reaches us, I wish to immediately raise the troops.
translations.idiomatic: Fearing exposure, the king told Wu Pi, "If it comes to the worst I mean to rise at once.

sentences.zh: 天下勞苦有間矣，諸侯頗有失行，皆自疑，我舉兵西鄉，必有應者；

translations.literal: The realm's toil and bitterness have had respite; the feudal lords have mostly misconducted themselves and all suspect each other; if I raise troops westward there will surely be those who respond;
translations.idiomatic: The people are weary and ripe for change; the regional kings stumble in their duties and mistrust one another. If I strike west, others will surely rise with me.

sentences.zh: 無應，即還略衡山。

translations.literal: If none respond, then return and raid Hengshan.
translations.idiomatic: If no one answers the call, I can still wheel about and overrun Hengshan."

sentences.zh: 勢不得不發。」

translations.literal: The situation compels that I cannot but rise.'
translations.idiomatic: The moment will leave me no choice."

sentences.zh: 被曰：「略衡山以擊廬江，有尋陽之船，守下雉之城，結九江之浦，絕豫章之口，強弩臨江而守，以禁南郡之下，東保會稽，南通勁越，屈強江、淮間，可以延歲月之壽耳，未見其福也。」

translations.literal: Pi said: 'Raid Hengshan to strike Lujiang, have ships of Xunyang, guard the city of Xiazhi, knot at the shores of Jiujiang, block the mouth of Yuzhang, strong crossbows face the river and guard, thereby forbid descent from Nanjun, east protect Kuaiji, south connect to strong Yue, stubborn between Jiang and Huai, can extend years and months of life only, not seen its blessing.'
translations.idiomatic: Wu Pi answered, "You might push from Hengshan into Lujiang, float Xunyang war junks, garrison Xiazhi, close the Jiujiang landings, seal Yuzhang's river mouth, line heavy crossbows along the bank to block a descent from Nanjun, shield Kuaiji on the east, and link arms with Yue in the south—stubbornly holding the Huai and Yangzi might buy you a little time, but I still see no blessing in it."

sentences.zh: 王曰：「左吳、趙賢、硃驕如皆以為什八九成，公獨以為無福，何？」

translations.literal: The king said: 'Zuo Wu, Zhao Xian, Zhu Jiaoru all thought eight or nine tenths success; you alone think no blessing—why?'
translations.idiomatic: The king said, "Zuo Wu, Zhao Xian, and Zhu Jiaoru all put our odds at eight or nine in ten. Why are you alone so bleak?"

sentences.zh: 被曰：「大王之群臣近幸素能使眾者，皆前系詔獄，余無可用者。」

translations.literal: Pi said: 'The great king's ministers and close favorites who in the past could command the multitudes have all earlier been bound in the imperial prison; the remainder are not usable.'
translations.idiomatic: "Your majesty's proven commanders are already in the imperial jail," said Wu Pi. "Who is left to lead?"

sentences.zh: 王曰：「陳勝、吳廣無立錐之地，百人之聚，起於大澤，奮臂大呼，天下響應，西至於戲而兵百二十萬。

translations.literal: The king said: 'Chen Sheng and Wu Guang had no land the size of a awl tip, a gathering of a hundred men, rose in the great marsh, waved arms and cried aloud, the realm responded like echo, west reached Xi and troops were 1,200,000.
translations.idiomatic: The king retorted, "Chen Sheng and Wu Guang held not an inch of soil—a hundred desperate men in a swamp. They waved their arms, shouted once, and a million and two hundred thousand answered them all the way to Xi.

sentences.zh: 今吾國雖小，勝兵可得二十萬，公何以言有禍無福？」

translations.literal: Now my state though small, crack troops can obtain two hundred thousand—why do you say there is misfortune and no blessing?'
translations.idiomatic: My kingdom is small, yet I can put two hundred thousand seasoned troops in the field—how can you speak only of disaster?"

sentences.zh: 被曰：「臣不敢避子胥之誅，願大王無為吳王之聽。

translations.literal: Pi said: 'Your servant does not dare evade Zixu's execution—wish the great king not to listen as the King of Wu listened.'
translations.idiomatic: "I do not fear sharing Zixu's fate," said Wu Pi, "but I beg you not to do as the king of Wu did and deafen yourself to counsel."

sentences.zh: 往者秦為無道，殘賊天下，殺術士，燔《詩》、《書》，滅聖跡，棄禮義，任刑法，轉海瀕之粟，致於西河。

translations.literal: Formerly Qin was without the Way, cruelly injured the realm, killed literati, burned the Odes and Documents, extinguished sage traces, cast aside ritual and right, relied on punishments and laws, transported grain from the seashores, brought it to West River.
translations.idiomatic: When Qin lost the Way it scourged the empire, murdered scholars, burned the classics, erased the traces of the sages, cast aside ritual for the whip and rod, and dragged grain by sea to feed the armies on the western river.

sentences.zh: 當是之時，男子疾耕不足於糧饋，女子紡績不足於蓋形。

translations.literal: At that time, men hastened to plow yet were not enough for grain transport; women spun and wove yet were not enough to cover forms.
translations.idiomatic: Men ploughed until they dropped yet could not fill the commissary trains; women spun until their fingers bled yet could not clothe the hosts.

sentences.zh: 遣蒙恬築長城，東西數千里。

translations.literal: Ordered Meng Tian to build the Long Wall, east-west several thousand li.
translations.idiomatic: The First Emperor sent Meng Tian to raise the Long Wall for thousands of li east to west.

sentences.zh: 暴兵露師，常數十萬，死者不可勝數，殭屍滿野，流血千里。

translations.literal: Exposed army in the open, always several ten thousands, dead not able to be counted, stiff corpses filled the wilds, flowing blood a thousand li.
translations.idiomatic: Armies camped in the open by the hundreds of thousands; the dead were beyond counting, corpses carpeted the fields, and blood ran for a thousand li.

sentences.zh: 於是百姓力屈，欲為亂者十室而五。

translations.literal: Then the hundred surnames' strength was bent; of those wishing to rebel, five in ten households.
translations.idiomatic: The common people were broken in body and spirit; half the households in the land were ready to rise.

sentences.zh: 又使徐福入海求仙藥，多繼珍寶，童男女三千人，五種百工而行。

translations.literal: Also sent Xu Fu to enter the sea seeking immortal drugs, carrying much rare treasure, three thousand boys and girls, five kinds of artisans, and went.
translations.idiomatic: Then he sent Xu Fu across the sea after elixirs, laden with treasure, with three thousand youths and maids and artisans of every trade.

sentences.zh: 徐福得平原大澤，止王不來。

translations.literal: Xu Fu obtained a broad plain great marsh, stopped and kinged, did not come.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Fu found wide plains overseas, declared himself king, and never sailed home.

sentences.zh: 於是百姓悲痛愁思，欲為亂者十室而六。

translations.literal: Thereupon the hundred surnames grieved and thought in sorrow; of those wishing to rebel, six in ten households.
translations.idiomatic: Grief and resentment spread; six households in ten were muttering rebellion.

sentences.zh: 又使尉佗逾五嶺，攻百越，尉佗知中國勞極，止王南越。

translations.literal: Also sent Wei Tuo to cross five ranges and attack Baiyue; Wei Tuo knew the Middle Kingdom was exhausted to the limit, stopped and kinged himself in Nanyue.
translations.idiomatic: He sent Wei Tuo over the Five Ridges against the Yue tribes; Wei Tuo saw how hollowed out the heartland had become, halted, and crowned himself king of Nanyue.

sentences.zh: 行者不還，往者莫返，於是百姓離心瓦解，欲為亂者十室而七。

translations.literal: Travelers did not return, those who went never came back—then the hundred surnames' hearts split and dissolved; of those wishing to rebel, seven in ten households.
translations.idiomatic: The conscripts never came home, the expeditions never returned, and loyalty to the throne crumbled until seven households in ten were muttering rebellion.

sentences.zh: 興萬乘之駕，作阿房之宮，收太半之賦，發閭左之戍。

translations.literal: Raised the chariot of ten thousand times, built Epang Palace, collected more than half taxes, called left-side of hamlet for corvee garrison.
translations.idiomatic: He ran the imperial carriages of state, raised Epang Palace, taxed the people beyond half their yield, and drafted even the paupers of the lanes for the frontier.

sentences.zh: 父不寧子，兄不安弟，政苛刑慘，民皆引領而望，傾耳而聽，悲號仰天，叩心怨上，欲為亂者，十室而八。

translations.literal: Fathers could not give sons peace, elder brothers could not give younger brothers peace, government harsh punishments cruel, the people all stretched necks and looked, inclined ears and listened, wailed to Heaven, knocked hearts resenting the superior; of those wishing to rebel, eight in ten households.
translations.idiomatic: Fathers could not rest for their sons, nor brothers for brothers; law grew crueler by the day. Every family craned its neck, strained its ears, beat its breast at Heaven, and cursed the throne—eight in ten were ready to revolt.

sentences.zh: 客謂高皇帝曰：『時可矣。』

translations.literal: A guest said to the High Emperor: "The time has come."
translations.idiomatic: A stranger said to Gaozu, "The hour has come."

sentences.zh: 高帝曰：『待之，聖人當起東南。』

translations.literal: The High Emperor said: "Wait—a sage will rise in the southeast."
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu answered, "Not yet. Heaven will first raise a sage in the southeast."

sentences.zh: 間不一歲，陳、吳大呼，劉、項並和，天下響應，所謂蹈瑕釁，因秦之亡時而動，百姓願之，若枯旱之望雨，故起於行陣之中，以成帝王之功。

translations.literal: Within less than a year Chen and Wu cried aloud, Liu and Xiang joined in harmony, the realm responded—it is what is called treading on cracks and fissures, moving by Qin's moment of perishing; the hundred surnames desired it like drought looking for rain; therefore rising from the ranks to complete emperor-king's merit.
translations.idiomatic: Within the year Chen Sheng and Wu Guang raised the cry; Liu Bang and Xiang Yu took up the tune; the empire answered like thunder. They stepped into the crack Qin had opened, struck when the dynasty was already dying, and rode a popular longing thick as drought for rain—rising from the ranks to forge an emperor's work.

sentences.zh: 今大王見高祖得天下之易也，獨不觀近世之吳、楚乎！

translations.literal: Now the great king sees how easy Gaozu obtained the realm—have you alone not observed recent Wu and Chu?'
translations.idiomatic: You think seizing the realm was easy for Gaozu—have you looked at what became of Wu and Chu?"

sentences.zh: 當今陛下臨制天下，一齊海內，泛愛蒸庶，布德施惠。

translations.literal: Now the present Son of Heaven holds all under heaven, unifies within the seas, broadly loves the steaming multitude, spreads virtue and shows kindness.
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven today holds the four seas in one hand, loves the common people like his own children, and rains down grace on every quarter.

sentences.zh: 口雖未言，聲疾雷震；

translations.literal: Though the mouth has not spoken, the sound is swift as thunder's crash;
translations.idiomatic: Though he has not spoken a word, his will rolls like thunder.

sentences.zh: 今雖未出，化馳如神。

translations.literal: though edicts have not yet issued, transforming influence gallops like spirit.'
translations.idiomatic: Though no edict has gone out, his transforming power already runs like a god's."

sentences.zh: 心有所懷，威動千里；

translations.literal: What the heart harbors, awe moves a thousand li;
translations.idiomatic: What the ruler feels in his breast can shake the realm a thousand li away;

sentences.zh: 下之應上，猶景響也。

translations.literal: those below responding to those above are like shadow and echo.
translations.idiomatic: and the people answer the throne as shadow answers form, as echo answers voice.

sentences.zh: 而大將軍材能非直章邯、楊熊也。

translations.literal: Moreover the Grand General's talent and ability are not merely those of Zhang Han and Yang Xiong.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand General is no Zhang Han or Yang Xiong—his gifts lie in another class entirely.

sentences.zh: 王以陳勝、吳廣論之，被以為過矣。

translations.literal: The great king taking Chen Sheng and Wu Guang as analogy—Pi thinks this is excessive error.
translations.idiomatic: To measure our chances by Chen Sheng and Wu Guang, as Your Highness does, is badly mistaken.

sentences.zh: 且大王之兵眾不能什分吳、楚之一，天下安寧又萬倍於秦時。

translations.literal: Moreover the great king's troops multitudes cannot reach one-tenth of Wu and Chu's; the realm's peace and security is again ten thousand times that of Qin times.
translations.idiomatic: Your hosts are not a tenth of what Wu and Chu fielded, while the empire today is ten thousand times more settled than under the First Emperor.

sentences.zh: 願王用臣之計。

translations.literal: I wish the king would use your servant's plan.
translations.idiomatic: I beg you to heed my counsel.

sentences.zh: 臣聞箕子過故國而悲，作《麥秀》之歌，痛紂之不用王子比干之言也。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that Jizi passed his old state and grieved, composed the song of the Wheat's Ears, pained that Zhou did not use Prince Bigan's words.
translations.idiomatic: When Jizi crossed the ruins of Yin he wept and sang The Wheat's Ears, mourning a king who would not hear Bigan.

sentences.zh: 故孟子曰，紂貴為天子，死曾不如匹夫。

translations.literal: Therefore Mencius said, Zhou though honored as Son of Heaven, in death was not even equal to a common man.
translations.idiomatic: Mencius said that though Zhou sat as Son of Heaven, he died with less dignity than a commoner.

sentences.zh: 是紂先自絕久矣，非死之日天去之也。

translations.literal: This was Zhou himself cutting off long before—not on the day of his death Heaven cast him off.
translations.idiomatic: He had already severed himself from Heaven long before the last day; Heaven did not abandon him in a single morning.

sentences.zh: 今臣亦竊悲大王棄千乘之君，將賜絕命之書，為群臣先，身死於東宮也。」

translations.literal: Now your servant also privately grieves that the great king discards a lord of a thousand chariots, about to receive the document granting death, becomes first among the ministers, body dying in the Eastern Palace.'
translations.idiomatic: I grieve to see you cast aside a kingdom of a thousand chariots, wait for the warrant that ends your line, go to the scaffold before your own courtiers, and breathe your last in the Eastern Palace you built for treason."

sentences.zh: 被因流涕而起。

translations.literal: Pi thereupon wept and rose.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Pi rose with tears streaming down his face.

sentences.zh: 後王復召問被：「苟如公言，不可以繳幸邪？」

translations.literal: Later the king again summoned and asked Pi: 'If it is as you say, cannot one seek a stroke of luck thereby?'
translations.idiomatic: Later the king called him back. "If matters stand as you say, is there no way to snatch a lucky break?"

sentences.zh: 被曰：「必不得已，被有愚計。」

translations.literal: Pi said: 'If there is no choice but necessity, Pi has a foolish plan.'
translations.idiomatic: "If we are driven to the wall," said Wu Pi, "I have a desperate stratagem."

sentences.zh: 王曰：「奈何？」

translations.literal: The king said: 'What is to be done?'
translations.idiomatic: "What is it?" asked the king.

sentences.zh: 被曰：「當今諸侯無異心，百姓無怨氣。

translations.literal: Pi said: 'At present the feudal lords have no different heart, the hundred surnames have no resentful qi.
translations.idiomatic: "The regional kings are loyal," said Wu Pi, "and the people bear the throne no grudge.

sentences.zh: 朔方之郡土地廣美，民徙者不足以實其地。

translations.literal: The commanderies of Shuofang have land broad and fine; the people who migrate are not enough to fill their ground.
translations.idiomatic: The northern commanderies around Shuofang are rich and empty—settlers have never filled them."

sentences.zh: 可為丞相、御史請書，徙郡國豪桀及耐罪以上，以赦令除，家產五十萬以上者，皆徙其家屬朔方之郡，益發甲卒，急其會日。

translations.literal: One can for the chancellor and imperial clerk request documents, move the braves of commanderies and kingdoms and those sentenced to tattooing and above, by amnesty edict remove their guilt, those whose household property is five hundred thousand cash and above—all move their families to Shuofang commanderies, further dispatch armored soldiers, hasten their assembly date.
translations.idiomatic: Forge orders from the chancellor and chief clerk: relocate every notable and every convict above the tattooing grade whose household is worth half a million cash, pardon them in the text of the edict, pack their kin to Shuofang, and call up extra troops on a short deadline.

sentences.zh: 又偽為左右都司空、上林中都官詔獄書，逮諸侯太子及幸臣。

translations.literal: Also forge documents of the left and right capital workmen and Shanglin central offices' imperial prison, arresting feudal lords' heirs and favored ministers.
translations.idiomatic: Forge warrants from the capital work bureaus and the Shanglin prison to seize crown princes and favorite ministers of the kingdoms.

sentences.zh: 如此，則民怨，諸侯懼，即使辯士隨而說之，黨可以徼幸。」

translations.literal: Like this, then the people will resent, the feudal lords will fear; even if you send persuaders to follow and speak to them, perhaps one may seek a thin stroke of luck.'
translations.idiomatic: Popular anger will flare, the kings will panic, and your rhetoricians might then squeeze a slim chance from the chaos."

sentences.zh: 王曰：「此可也。

translations.literal: The king said: 'This can be done.
translations.idiomatic: "That could work," said the king.

sentences.zh: 雖然，吾以不至若此，專發而已。」

translations.literal: Even so, I think it will not reach thus—I will simply raise the troops and that is all.'
translations.idiomatic: Still, I doubt we need go that far—a straight rising of arms will suffice."

sentences.zh: 後事發覺，被詣吏自告與淮南王謀反蹤跡如此。

translations.literal: Later when the affair was discovered, Pi went to the officials and personally reported that with the Prince of Huainan he plotted rebellion; the traces were thus.
translations.idiomatic: When the plot leaked, Wu Pi walked into the yamen and confessed every step of the treason he had plotted with the king of Huainan.

sentences.zh: 天子以伍被雅辭多引漢美，欲勿誅。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven because Wu Pi's elegant rhetoric much cited Han's beauties, wished not to execute him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was inclined to spare him: Wu Pi's testimony had praised the Han at every turn.

sentences.zh: 張湯進曰：「被首為王畫反計，罪無赦。」

translations.literal: Zhang Tang advanced, saying: 'Pi first for the king drew rebellious plans—crime without pardon.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Tang objected: "He was the first to draw up rebellion for his master—there can be no pardon."

sentences.zh: 遂誅被。

translations.literal: Thereupon Pi was executed.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Pi was put to death.

sentences.zh: 江充字次倩，趙國邯鄲人也。

translations.literal: Jiang Chong, style Ciqian, was a man of Handan in the state of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Jiang Chong, courtesy name Ciqian, came from Handan in Zhao.

sentences.zh: 充本名齊，有女弟善鼓琴歌舞，嫁之趙太子丹。

translations.literal: Chong's original name was Qi; he had a younger sister skilled at strumming the zither, singing and dancing, who married to Zhao heir Dan.
translations.idiomatic: His birth name was Zhao Qi; his younger sister was a gifted musician and dancer who became the concubine of Crown Prince Dan of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 齊得幸於敬肅王，為上客。

translations.literal: Qi obtained favor with King Jingsu and became an upper guest.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Qi won favor with King Jingsu of Zhao and was received as a honored retainer.

sentences.zh: 久之，太子疑齊以己陰私告王，與齊忤，使吏逐捕齊，不得，收系其父兄，按驗，皆棄市。

translations.literal: After a long time the heir suspected Qi had informed the king of his own secret shame, was at odds with Qi, ordered officials to pursue and capture Qi, could not obtain him, arrested and bound his father and elder brothers, examined and verified, all were executed in the market.
translations.idiomatic: In time the crown prince decided Qi had betrayed his secrets to the king, broke with him, and sent officers to hunt Qi down. When Qi vanished, they jailed his father and brothers, tortured confessions from them, and left them all exposed in the marketplace.

sentences.zh: 齊遂絕跡亡，西人關，更名充。

translations.literal: Qi thereupon cut traces and fled, entered the passes westward, changed name to Chong.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Qi vanished into the west, crossed the passes, and reemerged as Jiang Chong.

sentences.zh: 詣闕告太子丹與同產姊及王后宮奸亂，交通郡國豪猾，攻剽為奸，吏不能禁。

translations.literal: He went to the palace gate and reported Crown Prince Dan's illicit relations with his full sister and the ladies of the queen's palace, trafficking with the bravos of commanderies and kingdoms, raiding and plundering as outlaws, which officials could not restrain.
translations.idiomatic: At the palace gate he charged Prince Dan with incest with his half-sister and the harem women, with hiring ruffians across the realm for robbery and mayhem that local magistrates could not check.

sentences.zh: 書奏，天子怒，遣使者詔郡發吏卒圍趙王宮，收捕太子丹，移系魏郡詔獄，與廷尉雜治，法至死。

translations.literal: When the memorial was submitted, the Son of Heaven was angry, dispatched envoys edicting the commandery to raise officials and soldiers surrounding the Zhao king's palace, arrested Crown Prince Dan, transferred and bound him in Wei commandery imperial prison, jointly tried with the commandant of justice, by law to death.
translations.idiomatic: The memorial enraged the emperor, who ordered troops to seal the Zhao palace, seized Prince Dan, sent him to the imperial jail in Wei commandery for joint trial with the commandant of justice, and sought the death penalty.

sentences.zh: 趙王彭祖，帝異母兄也，上書訟太子罪，言「充逋逃小臣，苟為奸訛，激怒聖朝，欲取必於萬乘以復私怨。

translations.literal: King Pengzu of Zhao was the emperor's elder brother by a different mother; he submitted a memorial suing the heir's crimes, saying: 'Chong is a fugitive petty minister who recklessly practiced slander, enraged the sage court, wishing to force certainty from the ten-thousand chariots to repay private resentment.
translations.idiomatic: King Liu Pengzu of Zhao—half-brother to the emperor—filed a counter-memorial defending his son: "Jiang Chong is a runaway petty clerk spinning lies to bait the throne into doing his private vengeance.

sentences.zh: 後雖亨醢，計猶不悔。

translations.literal: Later though boiled and minced, the plan would still not regret.'
translations.idiomatic: Let him be boiled alive and he will still feel no remorse."

sentences.zh: 臣願選從趙國勇敢士，從軍擊匈奴，極盡死力，以贖丹罪。」

translations.literal: Your servant wishes to select brave men from Zhao to follow the army striking the Xiongnu, exhaust utmost death strength, thereby redeem Dan's crime.'
translations.idiomatic: I beg to lead Zhao's bravest against the Xiongnu and buy my son's life with blood."

sentences.zh: 上不許，竟敗趙太子。

translations.literal: The sovereign did not permit; in the end the Zhao heir was ruined.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor refused, and Prince Dan was destroyed in the end.

sentences.zh: 初，充召見犬台宮，自請願以所常被服冠見上。

translations.literal: At first Chong was summoned to audience at Quetai Palace, personally requesting to meet the sovereign in the cap and robes he habitually wore.
translations.idiomatic: When Jiang Chong was first summoned to Quetai Palace, he asked leave to appear in his own distinctive dress.

sentences.zh: 上許之。

translations.literal: The sovereign permitted it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor agreed.

sentences.zh: 充衣紗□禪衣，曲裾後垂交輸，冠禪纚步搖冠，飛翮之纓。

translations.literal: Chong clothed in gauze □ single-layer robe, curved lapel trailing behind crossed-weave, cap of fine mesh with swaying step-shake crown, tassels of flying wing feathers.
translations.idiomatic: He came in a sheer gauze gown (one character is missing in the text), a skirt whose panels crossed behind him, and a tall gauze cap hung with swaying kingfisher plumes.

sentences.zh: 充為人魁岸，容貌甚壯。

translations.literal: Chong as a man was towering; his appearance was very martial.
translations.idiomatic: He was a giant of a man, with a fierce, soldierly face.

sentences.zh: 帝望見而異之，謂左右曰：「燕、趙固多奇士。」

translations.literal: The emperor looked and saw him as strange, said to those at his side: 'Yan and Zhao indeed have many extraordinary gentlemen.'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor stared, then murmured to his attendants, "Yan and Zhao breed singular men."

sentences.zh: 既至前，問以當世政事，上說之。

translations.literal: When he had reached the front, asked him on current age government affairs, the sovereign was pleased with him.
translations.idiomatic: When Jiang Chong stepped forward, the emperor questioned him on policy and liked every answer.

sentences.zh: 充因自請，願使匈奴。

translations.literal: Chong thereupon personally requested, wished to be sent to the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: He volunteered for an embassy to the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 詔問其狀，充對曰：「因變制宜，以敵為師，事不可豫圖。」

translations.literal: Edicted to ask his views; Chong replied, saying: 'According with change to set what is fitting, take the enemy as teacher—affairs cannot be planned beforehand.'
translations.idiomatic: Asked how he would proceed, he said, "One must adapt to circumstance, learn from the foe, and never fix a plan in advance."

sentences.zh: 上以充為謁者使匈奴，還，拜為直指繡衣使者，督三輔盜賊，禁察逾侈。

translations.literal: The sovereign appointed Chong attendant envoy to the Xiongnu; on return, invested him as straight-pointing embroidered-clothes envoy, supervising capital-region bandits, forbidding and inspecting excess and arrogation.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor named him envoy to the Xiongnu, then on his return made him a straight-pointing embroidered-gown inspector with charge over capital banditry and sumptuary abuse.

sentences.zh: 貴戚近臣多奢僭，充皆舉劾，奏請沒入車馬，令身待北軍擊匈奴。

translations.literal: Noble in-laws and close ministers were mostly extravagant and usurping; Chong all impeached them, memorialized requesting confiscation of chariots and horses, ordering them personally to await the northern army striking the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: He impeached every powerful family and palace favorite for extravagance, seized their carriages and horses by memorial, and packed the owners off to serve in the northern army against the Xiongnu.
